bins.name: Modernize interconnection study methods
bins.desc: GridCARE argues that current study methods rely on static, worst-case assumptions that underestimate available grid capacity. The company supports study reforms that incorporate temporal, behavioral, and operational data so that planners can distinguish short-duration constraints from structural limitations and reduce unnecessary upgrade requirements.
quotes: longstanding planning and interconnection methodologies—relying heavily on static load profiles, worst-case peak assumptions, and deterministic single-snapshot analyses—no longer reflect how the modern grid operates nor how large loads actually behave.

planners can underestimate the hosting capacity that exists across the vast majority of hours. This gap between actual operational conditions and modeling assumptions contributes to the perception that only long-lead infrastructure can serve new load, when in many cases the constraint may be limited to a small number of hours or conditions.

utilities and RTOs lack standardized or consistent formats for customer-provided data, making it challenging to assess large numbers of requests or differentiate between short-duration constraints and structural limitations.

PJM's Critical Issue Fast Path, SPP's High-Impact Large Load process, and ISO-NE's ongoing work on load characterization all demonstrate that regions are exploring ways to streamline studies, incorporate temporal and behavioral data, better align study processes with actual system operations, and improve coordination with utilities.

the Commission could encourage or require transmission providers to publish transmission level hosting capacity maps or locational headroom indicators that give large load customers signals on where interconnection is most feasible.

bins.name: Expedited path for flexible loads
bins.desc: GridCARE supports an optional expedited study pathway for large loads that can commit to verifiable flexibility. The company cites emerging regional frameworks at PJM, SPP, and ISO-NE as evidence that this approach is workable and encourages FERC to establish a national framework allowing regional customization.
quotes: flexibility, when defined in measurable and verifiable terms, can help accelerate interconnections, reduce restudy risks, provide near-term operational headroom, and maintain reliability.

PJM's Critical Issue Fast Path, SPP's High-Impact Large Load process, and ISO-NE's ongoing work on load characterization all demonstrate that regions are exploring ways to streamline studies, incorporate temporal and behavioral data, better align study processes with actual system operations, and improve coordination with utilities.

Systematically incorporating this flexibility ecosystem into planning and operations will unlock existing capacity on the grid, accelerate interconnections, and enable beneficial load growth to preserve customer affordability and fund new infrastructure investment.

bins.name: Cost allocation and affordability protections
bins.desc: GridCARE contends that better identification of latent capacity and more precise load characterization can reduce unnecessary upgrade costs for existing customers. The company also supports tiered study fees and performance incentives for transmission providers as tools to align costs with system benefits.
quotes: planners can underestimate the hosting capacity that exists across the vast majority of hours. This gap between actual operational conditions and modeling assumptions contributes to the perception that only long-lead infrastructure can serve new load, when in many cases the constraint may be limited to a small number of hours or conditions.

Systematically incorporating this flexibility ecosystem into planning and operations will unlock existing capacity on the grid, accelerate interconnections, and enable beneficial load growth to preserve customer affordability and fund new infrastructure investment.
Applying generator-level deliverability assumptions to non-export configurations can overstate impacts, increase upgrade requirements, and erode the very flexibility the ANOPR seeks to unlock.

the Commission should establish a load-interconnection study-fee framework that scales with the size and complexity of the proposed load and reflects the level of operational flexibility demonstrated by the customer.
the Commission should consider performance-based incentives for transmission providers tied to each megawatt of load interconnected on an accelerated or performance-defined timeline.

A framework that better utilizes existing assets can serve near-term load, maintain affordability, and support the investment environment for utilities while long-lead projects progress through permitting and construction.

bins.name: Co-location and hybrid resource treatment
bins.desc: GridCARE urges FERC to study load and any co-located or behind-the-meter resources together, reflecting actual operating conditions. The company specifically argues that non-exporting behind-the-meter storage should be studied as a load-modifying resource rather than as a generator seeking firm deliverability.
quotes: The Commission should also support study approaches that clearly distinguish between non-exporting, load-modifying behind-the-meter resources and true generation resources seeking firm transmission rights.
Applying generator-level deliverability assumptions to non-export configurations can overstate impacts, increase upgrade requirements, and erode the very flexibility the ANOPR seeks to unlock.

bins.name: Latent capacity bridges near-term gap
bins.desc: GridCARE emphasizes that unlocking latent capacity on existing infrastructure can serve near-term large-load demand while long-lead transmission projects are still under development, complementing rather than replacing new investment.
quotes: A framework that better utilizes existing assets can serve near-term load, maintain affordability, and support the investment environment for utilities while long-lead projects progress through permitting and construction.

bins.name: Hybrid resource injection rights
bins.desc: GridCARE supports studying hybrid facilities according to the specific injection and withdrawal rights they request, consistent with DOE's proposed principle. Non-exporting configurations by definition request zero injection rights and should not trigger full deliverability study requirements.
quotes: The Commission should also support study approaches that clearly distinguish between non-exporting, load-modifying behind-the-meter resources and true generation resources seeking firm transmission rights.
Applying generator-level deliverability assumptions to non-export configurations can overstate impacts, increase upgrade requirements, and erode the very flexibility the ANOPR seeks to unlock.

bins.name: Expedited study pathway feasibility
bins.desc: GridCARE supports establishing an expedited or fast-track interconnection study pathway for loads that demonstrate verifiable flexibility, arguing that measurable flexibility commitments can reduce restudy risks and near-term constraints.
quotes: flexibility, when defined in measurable and verifiable terms, can help accelerate interconnections, reduce restudy risks, provide near-term operational headroom, and maintain reliability.

bins.name: Study fee structure for load interconnections
bins.desc: GridCARE recommends a load-interconnection study-fee framework that scales with load size and complexity and rewards demonstrated flexibility, analogous to the generator fee structure in Order No. 2023, to discourage speculative or inflated queue filings.
quotes: the Commission should establish a load-interconnection study-fee framework that scales with the size and complexity of the proposed load and reflects the level of operational flexibility demonstrated by the customer.

bins.name: PJM fast-path process as model
bins.desc: GridCARE notes PJM's Critical Issue Fast Path as an existing regional example of streamlined study processes that incorporate flexibility and behavioral data.
quotes: PJM's Critical Issue Fast Path, SPP's High-Impact Large Load process, and ISO-NE's ongoing work on load characterization all demonstrate that regions are exploring ways to streamline studies, incorporate temporal and behavioral data, better align study processes with actual system operations, and improve coordination with utilities.

bins.name: SPP large-load process as model
bins.desc: GridCARE cites SPP's High-Impact Large Load process as evidence that regions are already developing frameworks to accelerate interconnection for large loads.
quotes: PJM's Critical Issue Fast Path, SPP's High-Impact Large Load process, and ISO-NE's ongoing work on load characterization all demonstrate that regions are exploring ways to streamline studies, incorporate temporal and behavioral data, better align study processes with actual system operations, and improve coordination with utilities.

bins.name: ISO-NE load characterization work
bins.desc: GridCARE points to ISO-NE's ongoing load characterization work as a regional precedent for aligning study processes with actual system operations.
quotes: PJM's Critical Issue Fast Path, SPP's High-Impact Large Load process, and ISO-NE's ongoing work on load characterization all demonstrate that regions are exploring ways to streamline studies, incorporate temporal and behavioral data, better align study processes with actual system operations, and improve coordination with utilities.

bins.name: Outdated planning assumptions
bins.desc: GridCARE opposes continued reliance on static load profiles and worst-case deterministic analyses, arguing these methods were designed for centralized generation and no longer reflect modern large-load behavior.
quotes: longstanding planning and interconnection methodologies—relying heavily on static load profiles, worst-case peak assumptions, and deterministic single-snapshot analyses—no longer reflect how the modern grid operates nor how large loads actually behave.

bins.name: Unlock latent grid capacity
bins.desc: GridCARE's central argument is that significant transmission capacity already exists but goes unrecognized because of conservative modeling. Modernized planning and flexibility tools can surface this capacity to serve near-term load demand.
quotes: planners can underestimate the hosting capacity that exists across the vast majority of hours. This gap between actual operational conditions and modeling assumptions contributes to the perception that only long-lead infrastructure can serve new load, when in many cases the constraint may be limited to a small number of hours or conditions.

Systematically incorporating this flexibility ecosystem into planning and operations will unlock existing capacity on the grid, accelerate interconnections, and enable beneficial load growth to preserve customer affordability and fund new infrastructure investment.

A framework that better utilizes existing assets can serve near-term load, maintain affordability, and support the investment environment for utilities while long-lead projects progress through permitting and construction.

bins.name: Standardize customer data formats
bins.desc: GridCARE notes that the absence of standardized formats for customer-provided operational data makes it difficult for utilities to process large numbers of interconnection requests or distinguish transient constraints from structural ones.
quotes: utilities and RTOs lack standardized or consistent formats for customer-provided data, making it challenging to assess large numbers of requests or differentiate between short-duration constraints and structural limitations.

bins.name: Hosting capacity maps for siting
bins.desc: GridCARE recommends that transmission providers publish hosting capacity maps or locational headroom indicators so large-load customers can identify the most feasible interconnection sites and reduce queue uncertainty.
quotes: the Commission could encourage or require transmission providers to publish transmission level hosting capacity maps or locational headroom indicators that give large load customers signals on where interconnection is most feasible.

bins.name: Non-exporting resource classification
bins.desc: GridCARE argues that non-exporting behind-the-meter resources request zero injection rights and should be classified and studied as load-modifying resources, not as generators seeking firm deliverability.
quotes: The Commission should also support study approaches that clearly distinguish between non-exporting, load-modifying behind-the-meter resources and true generation resources seeking firm transmission rights.

bins.name: Load-based study fees
bins.desc: GridCARE recommends scaling interconnection study fees to load size, complexity, and demonstrated flexibility to deter speculative filings and align study resources with actual system impacts.
quotes: the Commission should establish a load-interconnection study-fee framework that scales with the size and complexity of the proposed load and reflects the level of operational flexibility demonstrated by the customer.

bins.name: Incentives for timely interconnection
bins.desc: GridCARE proposes performance-based incentives for transmission providers tied to each megawatt of load interconnected on an accelerated timeline, to encourage adoption of modernized analytical tools and flexibility pathways.
quotes: the Commission should consider performance-based incentives for transmission providers tied to each megawatt of load interconnected on an accelerated or performance-defined timeline.
